headline: CORN BREAKS 4% ON HOLIDAY EVE; OATS HIT -15%
subheadline: Markets closed Friday for Independence Day; Thursday's close left corn at $4.25, oats in freefall, and the July 4th weekend sitting on top of peak pollination.
lead: Corn closed Thursday at $4.25, down 4.0% in a single session, the biggest one-day drop in months for a crop that is right now, this weekend, pollinating across the Belt. Oats fell 15.5% to $2.86, a move that has no clean catalyst in Thursday's news and looks like fund liquidation in a thin pre-holiday session. Markets are closed today. The question that reopens Monday is whether the heat premium that built all week survived the selloff, or whether someone decided to cash it in before the long weekend.
the_takeaway: Corn sold off hard into peak pollination week. Monday's Crop Progress is the first chance to know why.
teaser: Corn's biggest drop in months landed the day before July 4th and right on top of pollination. Monday answers whether that made sense.

one_number.context: Oats fell $0.525 to $2.86 on Thursday, sitting at the 20th percentile of its 52-week range. There was no oats-specific news in Thursday's bucket. That kind of move with no catalyst is either a deeply illiquid pre-holiday market doing what thin markets do, or a structural signal about feed demand softening. At the 20% 52-week level, oats has now given back nearly everything it built since winter.

sections.title: Corn Sells Into Pollination
sections.body: Corn closed Thursday at **$4.25**, down 4.0%, the sharpest single-session drop of the summer so far, and it happened the day before a long weekend with the crop in tassel. December corn held far better, settling at **$4.42**, down only 0.3%, which tells you something: whoever sold nearby corn on Thursday was not making a new-crop call, they were getting out of a position. The NCGA dropped its 250-year corn demand roadmap Thursday, which is the kind of structural bullish story that does not move a July futures contract. What moves a July contract is heat, and the Belt has had plenty of it. USDA's acreage report, released Monday, confirmed more corn acres than March estimates, which added supply to the balance sheet right as the heat premium was building. That combination, more acres plus funds stepping out before a holiday, is the cleanest explanation for Thursday's break. December corn at $4.42 held, which means the new-crop market is not panicking. The new-crop market is waiting for Monday's Crop Progress to find out what three days of peak-pollination heat actually did.
sections.catalyst: USDA acreage confirmed above-estimate corn acres; pre-holiday fund liquidation in a quiet session.
sections.bottom_line: Nearby sold off hard; new-crop held. Separation says the market isn't calling a yield disaster yet.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: heat premium gave way to pre-holiday liquidation, exactly the risk flagged.

sections.title: Beans Hold Better Than Corn
sections.body: Soybeans nearby closed at **$11.32**, down 2.0%, and November beans held at **$11.48**, down only 0.6%. That spread matters. Nearby beans took a hit alongside corn in what was broadly a pre-holiday selloff, but new-crop beans are holding well above the levels that would signal a real demand problem. Soybean meal closed **up 0.7%** at $307.70 and soybean oil gained 2.2% to $66.95, so the crush complex is not telling the same story as the bean itself. China's $17 billion annual U.S. ag purchase commitment through 2028, announced May 18, has not produced sustained futures support, and Thursday's session was no exception. The beans story this weekend is the same as corn: peak July heat plus the acreage overhang from Monday's USDA report. The soyoil gain tells you renewable diesel demand is still present. The meal gain tells you crush is running. The bean price tells you the funds went home early.
sections.catalyst: Pre-holiday liquidation against Monday's above-estimate acreage numbers; crush complex diverged higher.
sections.bottom_line: Crush margins running fine; the bean selloff was funds, not fundamentals.
sections.vs_yesterday: Beans: export sales tone supported the hold call; November held above pressure zone.

sections.title: Livestock Fades Into Weekend
sections.body: Live cattle closed at **$239.23**, down 1.1%, and feeders settled at **$360.62**, down 1.0%. Hogs fell 3.2% to **$93.85**, the sharpest move in the livestock complex Thursday. The cattle close is consistent with what Brownfield reported: cash and wholesale pressure drove the week's fade, with August live cattle closing near $239.22 on Wednesday. The Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler plant lockout, ongoing since May 19, is still removing roughly 2% of weekly U.S. slaughter capacity, and that processing-constrained dynamic is keeping live cattle from finding a clean bid. Midyear auction data showed steer sales rising but heifer share dropping to 39%, and retention levels still fall short of signaling any herd expansion. The Korean beef market is opening a window for U.S. chilled beef as Australian imports push near safeguard limits, which is a real demand development, just not one that moves a Friday July contract. Hogs at $93.85 are sitting at the 50th percentile of their 52-week range with no fresh news behind Thursday's 3.2% drop. Watch that one Monday.
sections.catalyst: Cargill lockout processing constraint continues; cash and box-beef wholesale pressure; hogs fell without a clean catalyst.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle still locked in processing-constrained drift; hog drop needs a Monday explanation.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: narrative held exactly. Hogs broke harder than the drift pattern implied.

sections.title: Crude Firms; Geopolitics Goes Quiet
sections.body: WTI crude closed at **$68.78**, up 1.6%, and natural gas added 1.6% to **$3.25**. The energy complex moving opposite to grains on Thursday is worth clocking. Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, are producing exactly what an oil-price.com headline called Friday: markets growing numb to ceasefire drama. Crude is range-bound, the ceasefire is fragile and the shipping lanes are navigable, but TotalEnergies offering Iraqi barrels to Asian buyers at prompt delivery tells you supply is moving. OPEC production rebounded in June as Gulf producers brought shut-in barrels back online. The net result for U.S. farmers is that diesel input costs are not rising into harvest, which is the most useful thing crude can do right now. Gold at $4,187 and silver up 3.4% are telling a different story: the macro is not relaxed, the dollar slipped 0.5% to 100.83, and somebody is buying metal.
sections.catalyst: Iran-Hormuz ceasefire diplomacy advancing; OPEC June production rebound; TotalEnergies Iraqi crude flows to Asia.
sections.bottom_line: Diesel costs stable into harvest. Gold and silver say macro stress hasn't gone away.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Secretary Rollins Opposes Farmland Solar Conversion
outside_the_pit.body: Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins spoke against converting farmland to solar installations Thursday, a signal that the administration will not facilitate utility-scale solar buildout on productive ground. For producers fielding lease offers, this is the clearest policy read yet on where the department stands.

outside_the_pit.title: Korea Beef Safeguard Opens Door for U.S. Chilled Product
outside_the_pit.body: Australian beef imports hit over 90% of Korea's annual safeguard threshold, which triggers price premiums on additional imports and makes U.S. chilled beef more competitive at retail and in restaurants. It is a real demand window that doesn't show up in today's futures close but will show up in export sales data over the next few weeks.

outside_the_pit.title: USMCA Renegotiation Draws Bipartisan Ag Concern
outside_the_pit.body: House Agriculture Committee Democrats and agriculture groups formally expressed concern Thursday over President Trump's decision not to renew USMCA, opting instead for fresh bilateral negotiations. For livestock and specialty crop producers, Canada and Mexico are the two largest export markets, and any negotiating gap creates real uncertainty on market access.

the_more_you_know.title: 39%: The Heifer Number That Says Herd Rebuilding Isn't Here Yet
the_more_you_know.body: Midyear 2026 auction data released Thursday showed heifer share of steer sales at 39%, a number worth understanding. When heifer retention rises, producers are keeping females back to rebuild cow herds, which pulls supply from the market and eventually tightens the cattle cycle. At 39%, heifer share is not sending that signal. Retention levels are still below the threshold historically associated with expansion cycles, meaning the U.S. cow herd is not growing yet. For live cattle at $239.23, sitting at the 64th percentile of its 52-week range, that structural tightness is the ceiling argument. The processing constraint from the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout, removing roughly 2% of weekly slaughter capacity since May 19, is the floor argument. The market is caught between both.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn pollination rating is the number. Any deterioration in good/excellent from last week's reading is the first hard evidence of whether the July 4th weekend heat did damage. Good/excellent below 65% adds weather premium back into December corn at $4.42.

Watch December corn open: if it gaps toward $4.50 or higher on Crop Progress leak or weather model changes over the weekend, the Thursday selloff was a buying opportunity. If it opens flat or lower, the fund liquidation story has more room to run.
Weekly Export Sales: soybeans below 300,000 MT keeps November beans at $11.48 under pressure. Above 500,000 MT and the China commitment starts showing up as sustained flow, not just a headline.

WASDE monthly report: July WASDE will carry USDA's first look at 2026 crop yield estimates incorporating early-season heat. Any yield cut from the 181 bu/acre trendline in corn is the number that resets the whole supply story.

daily_quote.text: He who knows when he can fight and when he cannot, will be victorious.

source_summary: CME Group settlement prices; USDA Crop Progress; USDA Acreage Report June 30, 2026; Brownfield Ag News; Feedstuffs; Beef Magazine; OilPrice.com; The Fence Post
